channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - kotaku.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for kotaku.com

item.title: Let’s Rank The Pokémon Starters, Worst To Best
item.content:encoded:
Everyone has a favorite starter Pokémon. They’re the first critters we begin our journey with in each game, and they hold a special place in our hearts long after we move on to the next game. Can we really quantify how special these little friends are and sand down all that love and meaning into something as arbitrary as a ranking? Yes, and we’re doing it. So strap the fuck in.

We originally published this ranking in 2023, but now that we know the starters for Pokémon Winds and Waves, we can slot the new trio in based on what we know so far. Will they move up or down when the games finally launch in 2027? Probably! But for now, here’s where they’re standing against the nine generations that came before.

Also, this is just a ranking of the first stages of these Pokémon. So don’t yell at me about Charizard. He’s not here.



https://kotaku.com/pokemon-new-gen-starters-best-worst-ranked-winds-waves-1850287696
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Resident Evil 4 Remake’s Performance Ruining DRM Removed After Less Than 30 Days
item.content:encoded:
Last month, Capcom swapped out the digital rights management (DRM) software in 2023’s Resident Evil 4 remake on Steam. The new DRM caused massive performance problems, upsetting players who had been playing the game fine until then. Capcom has seemingly realized this was a dumb decision and has now quietly removed the anti-tamper DRM from RE4.

In early February, without any warning or heads up, Capcom removed Denuvo DRM from the Resident Evil 4 remake and replaced it with Enigma, a DRM solution that is even more hated than the controversial Denuvo. And almost immediately after making this change to the PC version of RE4, players began noticing performance drops that hadn’t been happening before. YouTuber ItalicMaze used a mod to restore the game’s previous version on Steam and then compared how the game runs with and without Enigma. The results were bad, with the new DRM seemingly causing significant FPS drops, especially during action-heavy moments. Digital Foundry did similar testing and found a roughly 20-percent drop in performance. Ouch. People online yelled at Capcom a lot about this, and now the publisher has gotten the message and ripped the new DRM out.

On March 3, just 28 days after updating RE4 and adding Enigma, Capcom updated the game again, this time removing the new DRM from it. This can be spotted via SteamDB’s changelog for Resident Evil 4 remake. It now seems like RE4 on Steam has no DRM.

Kotaku has reached out to Capcom to get confirmation of Enigma’s removal and to find out if it could return.

This isn’t the first time a Resident Evil game has featured DRM that ruins performance. Something similar happened with Resident Evil Village in 2021. Pirated versions of the horror sequel reportedly ran better than the official Steam release because they lacked DRM. Two years later, in April 2023, Capcom removed Denuvo from Resident Evil Village. It didn’t replace it with a new DRM as it did with RE4 last month.

Hopefully, this whole RE4 situation was a test, and the resulting bad press and terrible performance have scared Capcom away from repeating it with older PC games in the future.



https://kotaku.com/resident-evil-4-remake-denuvo-enigma-drm-removed-steam-pc-28-days-capcom-performance-2000675382
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Resident Evil Requiem’s Arachnophobia Mods Now Include One That Replaces Spiders With Deadly Tank Engine
item.content:encoded:
After I played through Resident Evil Requiem for the first time, I warned a friend of mine with debilitating arachnophobia that there was a segment about midway through Capcom’s latest horror game that is so spider-heavy that he might as well throw any thought of playing the game out of his mind forever. But luckily, modders foresaw this, and have already put out arachnophobia mods for the game.The ways in which these mods go about removing spiders from the game range from strictly practical to completely absurd.

Mods to remove spiders from games are pretty common because it turns out spiders themselves show up in a lot of games, and for some players, their presence can be a dealbreaker. Whether they’re low-level enemies in an RPG dungeon or giant bosses like in Requiem, if your phobia is bad enough, it can keep you from playing a game entirely. So when a game like Requiem puts a major focus on the eight-legged creepy crawlers, Capcom has to know this will nudge at least a few people out of the pool. Well, mere days after the game’s  February 27 launch, modders have already given those players who want to see Grace and Leon’s story through without fighting a giant spider a few options.

One mod on Nexus Mods replaces the big spider Leon fights in Requiem’s second act with “formless blobs.” Another swaps the models for the various arachnids with different creature models, including birds, rats, and even the big guy himself, Mr. X. The most humorous one is a mod that turns the big spider into Thomas the Tank Engine, which could be scarier than a giant spider depending on who you are. Resident Evil fans have looked to the sentient train to alleviate some of the horrors before, most notably by modding out Resident Evil 2‘s Mr. X for the train back in 2019.

Ultimately, the spider segments in Resident Evil Requiem are pretty short, but if the threat of an eight-legged bug is enough to make you skip a game entirely, there are now at least a few options for you on PC. Now you can just be scared by all the other horrifying monsters in Grace and Leon’s way like the rest of us.



https://kotaku.com/resident-evil-requiem-arachnophobia-mod-thomas-tank-2000675379
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
